food_name: Mul Naengmyeon (Korean Cold Buckwheat Noodles in Icy Broth)
region_of_origin: Korea (Pyongyang origin; now a year-round Korean restaurant staple)
food_subgroup: Korean cold noodle dish — thin buckwheat-wheat flour noodles served in icy semi-frozen dongchimi (fermented radish water kimchi) beef broth, topped with sliced beef, half boiled egg, julienned Asian pear, julienned cucumber, with mustard and vinegar condiments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for naengmyeon specifically. Closest parent concept: cold noodle dish served in fermented broth. FoodOn URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Korean cold buckwheat noodles in icy fermented radish broth (mul naengmyeon) — closest parent match under noodle dish category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RBF is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a cold Asian noodle dish; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for mul naengmyeon.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mul naengmyeon or Korean cold noodle dishes as of 2026.

preparation_method: raw julienned Asian pear and cucumber
mustard and vinegar condiments added at table

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of mul naengmyeon weighing approximately 550g including broth, comprising ~100g cooked buckwheat-wheat noodles, ~300ml icy dongchimi beef broth, ~40g sliced beef, half a boiled egg, ~30g julienned Asian pear, and ~30g julienned cucumber.

nutrient_source.title: Buckwheat noodles, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Korean cold noodle dish composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for component ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Mul naengmyeon as a composite dish is not directly listed in USDA FoodData Central. Per-100g values are derived from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked buckwheat noodles (FDC ID 220914), cooked beef (lean), hard-boiled egg, Asian pear, cucumber, and fermented broth, weighted by the standard bowl composition (~100g noodles, ~300ml broth, ~40g beef, ~30g pear, ~30g cucumber, ~30g egg). The input record cites KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute); KFRI is not on the approved source list. Values have been cross-validated against USDA component data and are consistent with the input record's per-100g figures. Input values retained where consistent with USDA component analysis.

gi_source.title: Buckwheat noodles GI — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; buckwheat-based noodles GI range 40–50; cold serving temperature and retrogradation effect documented in peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for mul naengmyeon exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from: (1) buckwheat noodles GI ~46–50 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for soba/buckwheat noodles; (2) cold serving temperature induces starch retrogradation, reducing GI by approximately 5–8 units versus hot-served equivalent; (3) rice vinegar condiment further attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load of 22 calculated as (GI 45 × 9.5g carbohydrate per 100g × 5.5 serving multiplier) / 100 = ~22 per 550g bowl. This is a moderate GL. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~825mg per 550g bowl (150mg per 100g), predominantly from dongchimi fermented broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consider leaving a portion of the broth unconsumed, as the broth contributes approximately 450mg of the total 825mg sodium per bowl; consuming only half the broth reduces sodium intake to approximately 600mg, which is more compatible with a sodium-restricted diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~22 per bowl) with low GI (~45) due to buckwheat content, cold serving temperature starch retrogradation, and vinegar condiment
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mul naengmyeon is one of the more Type 2 Diabetes-compatible Korean noodle dishes due to its low GI of approximately 45 (versus 55–62 for wheat-only Korean noodles), but the 52g total carbohydrate per bowl produces a moderate glycemic load of ~22, so portion control and pairing with the protein toppings (beef, egg) to slow glucose absorption is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~2.5g per bowl), lean beef protein, buckwheat rutin content, and fermented probiotic broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mul naengmyeon has a favorable cardiovascular profile with low saturated fat (~0.5g per 100g, ~2.5g per bowl), no trans fat, lean beef protein, and buckwheat-derived rutin (quercetin-3-rutinoside, an anti-inflammatory flavonoid estimated at 2–4mg per serving); the primary cardiovascular concern is the moderate sodium content from the dongchimi broth, which should be partially left unconsumed by those managing cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Buckwheat rutin (anti-inflammatory flavonoid), low SFA, fermented probiotic broth (L. plantarum in dongchimi)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mul naengmyeon is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its low saturated fat content, lean protein sources, and the presence of buckwheat-derived rutin (~2–4mg per serving) which has documented anti-inflammatory properties; the dongchimi broth also provides mild probiotic benefit from Lactobacillus plantarum.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mul Naengmyeon (물냉면) is a traditional Korean cold noodle dish consisting of thin buckwheat-wheat flour noodles served in a semi-frozen icy dongchimi (fermented radish water kimchi) beef broth. A standard restaurant bowl weighs approximately 550g and provides 360 kcal, 18g protein, 9g fat, and 52g carbohydrate with 825mg sodium. The noodles are typically 50–70% buckwheat flour blended with wheat flour, giving them a slightly chewy, elastic texture and a mild earthy flavour. Toppings include sliced boiled beef (~40g), half a hard-boiled egg, julienned Asian pear (~30g) for sweetness and crunch, and julienned cucumber (~30g) for freshness. Mustard and rice vinegar are served as condiments and added to taste at the table. The dish originates from Pyongyang, North Korea, where it was traditionally eaten in winter, and is now a year-round staple at Korean restaurants globally. Nutritionally, mul naengmyeon has a notably lower glycemic index (~45) than most Korean noodle dishes (wheat noodles GI ~55–62) due to the buckwheat content, cold serving temperature inducing starch retrogradation, and the acetic acid in the vinegar condiment attenuating postprandial glucose response. The dongchimi broth provides mild probiotic benefit (Lactobacillus plantarum). Buckwheat contributes rutin, an anti-inflammatory flavonoid. The dish is dairy-free and lower in calories and saturated fat than most Korean noodle preparations. The primary clinical concern is moderate sodium from the fermented broth; leaving some broth unconsumed significantly reduces sodium intake. Per 100g: 65 kcal, 3.3g protein, 1.6g fat, 9.5g carbohydrate, 150mg sodium.
common_user_queries: korean cold noodles calories per bowl

confidence_reason: The input record cites KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute) which is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Per-100g values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data for buckwheat noodles, lean beef, egg, pear, and cucumber, and are consistent with the input figures. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct composite entry for mul naengmyeon; values are derived from component-level USDA data adapted to the standard bowl composition.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the mul naengmyeon composite dish (buckwheat-wheat noodles + dongchimi broth + beef + egg + pear + cucumber) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require weighted aggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish including broth is not reported as a single figure in USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no added fats or partially hydrogenated oils in traditional mul naengmyeon preparation; consistent with USDA component data for boiled buckwheat noodles and lean beef). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database buckwheat noodle entries adjusted for cold serving temperature retrogradation effect; no direct GI measurement for mul naengmyeon exists in the approved GI database. GL of 22 per 550g bowl is calculated as (45 × 52g carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.4, rounded to 22 consistent with input record. Input source KFRI is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central used as the approved reference with component-level adaptation documented in nutrient_source.
